does: This paper develops a Transcoder-based interpretability framework to understand how vision-language models transform visual inputs into text. Rather than analyzing static residual representations like prior sparse autoencoders, the authors focus on MLP sublayers as causal computational units, treating Transcoders as sparse approximations that serve as functional proxies. Applied to Gemma 3-4B-IT, the framework decomposes the model into interpretable pathways that trace how image patches drive token generation. The authors validate their approach through patch ablation experiments, showing that Transcoder attributions produce stronger and more stable effects on visually grounded tokens compared to SAE attributions and better align with semantically relevant image regions. Through false visual grounding counterfactuals, they confirm these pathways specifically mediate vision-language interaction rather than generic computation.
connects: This work extends the interpretability literature on sparse autoencoders and mechanistic understanding of large language models by shifting focus from static representation analysis to dynamic computation. It builds on prior SAE-based interpretability methods applied to multimodal systems but addresses their limitation of missing layer-wise functional updates. The approach connects to broader circuit analysis work that traces causal pathways through neural networks, adapting these techniques to the vision-language domain. The paper also engages with existing work on hallucinations in generative models and their structural properties, combining classical circuit-discovery methods with multimodal analysis to understand systematic failure modes in VLMs.
enables: This framework opens several research directions for improving VLM interpretability and reliability. The Transcoder methodology could be extended to larger and more capable vision-language models, potentially revealing scaling laws in cross-modal reasoning. By identifying specific computational pathways responsible for hallucinations through graph-based circuit analysis, the work suggests interventions for reducing false visual grounding—for instance, through targeted suppression of identified spurious pathways or architectural modifications. The function-centric interpretability approach could inform better training procedures and alignment techniques for VLMs. More broadly, the demonstrated method provides a foundation for auditing visual reasoning in multimodal systems and could support development of more trustworthy vision-language models for downstream applications.
